dailyJudgementSnippet: Google Gemini wins the day with the highest positive score (0.110), helped by praise for benchmark performance and optimism about Google’s AI trajectory [E9][E10][E11]. Anthropic was mildly positive but held back by pricing and usage-limit complaints [E5][E8], while OpenAI and especially Microsoft Copilot were net negative, with Copilot dragged down by strong backlash to usage-based billing and perceived product decline [E13][E14].
winnerExplanation: Google Gemini was the clear sentiment winner in this frontpage snapshot: its score of 0.110 led Anthropic at 0.058 by about 0.052, with no low-confidence or close-call flag. Its positive drivers centered on strong reported benchmark results and favorable perceptions of Google’s AI momentum [E9][E10][E11], despite at least one direct negative comparison calling Gemini less useful [E12]. Anthropic finished second with modestly positive overall sentiment, balancing praise for Claude/Opus effectiveness [E6] against complaints about pricing, subscriptions, and reduced limits [E5][E7][E8]. OpenAI was substantially negative overall, with criticism of access limits, AGI rhetoric, and financing strategy outweighing positives around expanded availability and favorable agreements [E1][E2][E3][E4]. Microsoft Copilot was the weakest by a wide margin, driven by heavy criticism of billing changes, multipliers, higher prices, and perceived quality decline [E13][E14][E15][E16].

openai.judgementSnippet: OpenAI had a negative day: 259 mentions with more negatives than positives, yielding a score of -0.316. Positive discussion noted broader availability and favorable agreements [E2][E4], but criticism focused on blocked third-party agents, low API limits, AGI rhetoric, and concerns over the Azure/OpenAI financing loop [E1][E3].

anthropic.judgementSnippet: Anthropic was mildly positive overall, scoring 0.058 across 130 mentions. Praise for Claude Code and Opus effectiveness helped [E6], but the upside was limited by direct complaints about model pricing, subscription-style billing, reduced Claude usage limits, and cost transparency [E5][E7][E8].

google_gemini.judgementSnippet: Google Gemini led the field with a 0.110 score from 76 mentions. The positive case came from benchmark praise and optimism that Google is positioned strongly in AI [E9][E10][E11]. There was some criticism, including a direct unfavorable comparison calling Gemini less useful [E12], but it was not enough to offset the net positive discussion.

microsoft_copilot.judgementSnippet: Microsoft Copilot had the worst sentiment by far, scoring -0.796 across 347 mentions. The discussion was dominated by anger over usage-based billing, reduced limits, model multipliers, higher effective pricing, and perceived product deterioration [E13][E14][E15][E16].

evidence.summary: The commenter says OpenAI login works, but then explicitly blames OpenAI for blocking third-party agents and reports errors/very low API rate limits for GPT models.
The comment frames OpenAI model availability on Bedrock as the main upside and says it is 'huge,' indicating clear approval of OpenAI's expanded availability/procurement options.
Criticizes OpenAI’s AGI rhetoric and describes its Azure/OpenAI financing loop as “shocking” and “circular nonsense,” framing the company’s strategy negatively.
Calls the agreement 'so friendly towards OpenAI' and says the prior agreement was 'kneecapping OpenAI' and putting its investment at risk.

Direct complaint about Anthropic model pricing: the commenter says Sonnet and Opus turns used to cost less and now cost much more, calling the change unfair and 'stealing.' This is explicitly aimed at the model/provider pricing, not a reseller wrapper.
Praises Claude Code and the underlying Opus models as the most effective option compared with other approaches tried.
Directly pairs Anthropic with OpenAI as part of the criticized subscription-based pricing model and says it 'won't do it for ai.'
Anthropic is directly criticized for reduced Claude usage limits and also lumped into the complaint about poor cost transparency.
Praises the reported benchmark results as impressive and congratulates the work; no criticism of Gemini itself, and the harness remark is neutral toward the model provider.

The post reports an OSS agent built on Gemini-3-flash-preview scoring well on TerminalBench, which reflects positively on Gemini’s model quality. The lower 'google's official 47.8%' is mentioned as a benchmark comparison, but the overall framing is more about the strong result than a criticism of Gemini itself.
The commenter expresses strong optimism about Google, saying it is 'cooking' and will 'win the ai game,' which reads as a positive judgment toward Google Gemini/the company’s AI direction.
The commenter explicitly compares it unfavorably to the other system, saying Gemini was 'quite useless' and less censored. This is a direct negative judgment of Gemini’s usefulness and behavior.
The comment strongly condemns GitHub Copilot’s shift to usage-based billing, calling the reduced limits unfair, warning annual subscribers were misled, and urging legal action/regulation.
The commenter says GitHub Copilot has been an "absolute shitshow," claims the AI is "weaker / dumber," and says prices increased "manyfold," expressing strong negative sentiment toward the product.
The comment strongly criticizes GitHub Copilot's new usage-based billing and model multipliers as the end of subsidized inference, and says users would be better off with OpenRouter.

The comment says Copilot’s pricing is 'no longer sustainable,' points to a '9x multiplier,' and questions 'why use Copilot to begin with,' explicitly comparing it unfavorably to buying tokens directly.
